source: Invest Like the Best — The cyclone under the surface (EP.260)
summary: Free cash flow generation is the key differentiator within growth tech in the current environment; market is beginning to price this distinction
summary_extended: Baker argues that free cash flow generation has become the critical differentiator within high-growth technology stocks in early 2022, drawing a sharp line between companies trading at 2-4% FCF yield on 2023 estimates versus cash-burning peers with no near-term path to profitability. He observes that prior to the interview, the market had been indiscriminately selling anything trading above 10-15x sales in a 'correlation one' fashion, but that in the week of the interview, the market began meaningfully separating FCF-generative growth companies from cash burners. Baker's reasoning is structural: in a rising rate environment, the duration of cash flows matters enormously, and companies that can demonstrate real near-term FCF are fundamentally less risky than those dependent on terminal value assumptions far in the future. The investment implication is to favor high-growth software and tech names that can demonstrate 2-4% FCF yields on 2023 numbers, as these are likely to outperform cash-burning peers even if both trade at elevated revenue multiples.
detail: Baker has a 'big line in the sand' around free cash flow generation. Companies at 2-4% FCF yield looking to 2023 are fundamentally different from cash-burning peers. He notes the market began differentiating on this basis in the week prior to the interview, moving away from the prior 'correlation one' sell-offs where everything over 10-15x sales fell equally.
confidence_evidence: Baker describes this as a 'big line in the sand' and observes real-time market behavior confirming the differentiation.
quote_fragment: big line in the sand for me...two, two and a half, 3% free cash flow yield looking out to 23
